figures.caption: Table 4: Performance of the proposed Histo-MExNet framework under SameMagnification (Type 1) and Cross-Magnification (Type 2) experimental settings.
Figure 3: t-SNE visualization comparing how well the baseline CNNs and the proposed model using the DenseNet201 backbone separate features at a 100× magnification.

Table 7: Comparative analysis of classification accuracy (%) between existing state-ofthe-art methods and the proposed Histo-MExNet across four individual magnifications (40×, 100×, 200×, 400×) and the combined all-magnification setting.

Figure 2: Overview of the proposed Histo-MExNet framework.Features are extracted using DenseNet201, the most effective backbone, as part with CBAM attention. These features are mapped to a prototype-aligned space and processed by several expert classifiers. The outputs of these classifiers are adaptively fused by a gating network to produce the final prediction.

Table 3: Comparison between baseline CNN architectures evaluated under Type 1 experimental settings, where models are trained and tested on the same magnification level using a random split on each type of magnification.
Figure 5: Confusion matrix for the DenseNet201 backbone under Type 3 experimental condition, trained on mixed data from all magnifications and assessed on a stratified test split.

Table 8: Ablation study of Histo-MExNet evaluating the impact of key components on classification performance. The table reports Accuracy, Weighted Precision, Weighted Recall, and Weighted F1-Score for the full model and variants with specific components removed, including ensemble learning (A1), hybrid loss (A2), attention module (A3), and prototype module (A4). This analysis highlights the individual contribution of each module to the overall model effectiveness.
Table 2: Overview of the benign and malignant histopathological subtypes represented in the BreaKHis dataset.

Figure 1: Representative subset of the BreaKHis dataset illustrating all eight tumor subtypes across four magnification levels (40×, 100×, 200×, and 400×). Each column stands for a particular form of tumor, and each row stands for a different level of magnification. This shows how tissue morphology and cellular detail change at different scales.
Table 6: Quantitative Analysis of Occlusion Sensitivity Features by Class and Magnifications

Figure 6: Occlusion sensitivity study in Ductal Carcinoma. The rows indicate magnification levels of 40×, 100×, 200×, and 400×. Columns display the input picture, occlusion sensitivity map, and overlay. The red zones emphasize the individual tissue properties that have the most influence on the model’s categorization choice, with confidence ratings for each scale.
